```
│  │ WASM     │ │ (provider + account)     │  │
│  └──────────┘ └──────────────────────────┘  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Technology Stack

| Layer | Technology |
|-------|-----------|
| Framework | Next.js 15 (App Router) |
| Runtime | Bun |
| State | Effect Atoms (`@effect-atom/atom-react`) |
| Services | Effect-TS (`Effect.Service`, `Schema.TaggedError`) |
| Styling | Tailwind CSS v4 |
| Crypto | falcon-rs compiled to WASM via `wasm-pack` |
| Starknet | `starknet.js` (RPC provider, account) |
| Network | Starknet Mainnet |
| Deploy | Vercel |

## Effect Services

### FalconService

Wraps the falcon-rs WASM module. Depends on `WasmRuntime` service (lazy WASM loader).

```typescript
class FalconService extends Effect.Service<FalconService>()("FalconService", {
  accessors: true,
  dependencies: [WasmRuntime.Default],
  effect: Effect.gen(function* () {
    const wasm = yield* WasmRuntime
    return {
      generateKeypair: Effect.fn("Falcon.generateKeypair")(function* () { ... }),
      sign: Effect.fn("Falcon.sign")(function* (message: Uint8Array, secretKey: SecretKey) { ... }),
      createVerificationHint: Effect.fn("Falcon.createHint")(function* (s1, pkNtt) { ... }),
      packPublicKey: Effect.fn("Falcon.packPublicKey")(function* (pk: PublicKey) { ... }),
    }
  })
}) {}
```

### StarknetService

Wraps starknet.js for mainnet interaction.

```typescript
class StarknetService extends Effect.Service<StarknetService>()("StarknetService", {
  accessors: true,
  effect: Effect.gen(function* () {
    const rpcUrl = yield* Config.string("NEXT_PUBLIC_STARKNET_RPC_URL")
    return {
      deployAccount: Effect.fn("Starknet.deployAccount")(function* (pk: PackedPublicKey) { ... }),
      sendTransaction: Effect.fn("Starknet.sendTransaction")(function* (tx: Transaction) { ... }),
      waitForTx: Effect.fn("Starknet.waitForTx")(function* (hash: TxHash) { ... }),
    }
  })
}) {}
```

### Errors

All errors use `Schema.TaggedError` with specific types per failure mode:

- `WasmLoadError` — WASM module failed to load
- `KeygenError` — Keypair generation failed
- `SigningError` — Message signing failed
- `VerificationFailedError` — Verification returned false (with step info)
- `StarknetRpcError` — RPC call failed (with code)
- `AccountDeployError` — Account deployment failed (with txHash if available)
- `InsufficientFundsError` — Account not funded for deployment

### Atoms

```typescript
const wasmStatusAtom = Atom.make<"loading" | "ready" | "error">("loading")
const keypairAtom = Atom.make<Option<FalconKeypair>>(Option.none())
const verificationAtom = Atom.make<VerificationState>({ step: "idle" })
const starknetAtom = Atom.make<Option<StarknetConnection>>(Option.none())
const pipelineStepsAtom = Atom.make<Array<PipelineStep>>([])
```

## Page Structure

Single-page scrolling layout. Interactive sections lazy-loaded via `next/dynamic`.

```
/ (landing)
  ├── Hero (RSC) — headline, tagline, key performance stats
  ├── Why Post-Quantum (RSC) — explainer with diagrams
  ├── Performance Stats (RSC) — step counts, gas costs, comparison table
  ├── Live Verification (Client) — interactive playground
  ├── Pipeline Deep-Dive (Client) — animated step-through visualizer
  ├── Deploy Account (Client) — mainnet deployment walkthrough
  └── Footer (RSC) — links, GitHub, docs
```

### Hero Section (RSC)

- "Post-Quantum Signatures on Starknet" headline
- Key numbers: 63K steps, 62 calldata felts, 29 storage slots
- CTA buttons: "Try Verification" → scroll, "Deploy Account" → scroll

### Verification Playground (Client)

- Text input for message
- "Generate Keypair" → `FalconService.generateKeypair`
- "Sign & Verify" → full pipeline:
  1. Sign message → `(s1, salt)`
  2. Create hint → `mul_hint`
  3. Pack inputs → 62 felt252 calldata
  4. Display packed representation
- Result: green checkmark / red X with details

### Pipeline Visualizer (Client)

Animated step-through of the verification algorithm:

1. `hash_to_point(msg || salt)` → 512 Zq coefficients
2. `NTT(s1)` → frequency domain
3. Pointwise multiply `NTT(s1) * pk_ntt`
4. `NTT(mul_hint)` → verify matches product
5. `s0 = msg_point - mul_hint` → recover s0
6. Norm check: `||s0||² + ||s1||² ≤ bound`

Each step shows: input → operation → output (truncated), step count.
Play/pause/step controls.

### Account Deploy Flow (Client)

- Step 1: Generate or reuse keypair
- Step 2: Pack public key → 29 felt252 display
- Step 3: Compute counterfactual deploy address
- Step 4: Fund account (show address, wait for balance)
- Step 5: Deploy → real mainnet transaction
- Step 6: Sign a test transaction with the new account
- Transaction links to Starkscan/Voyager

## WASM Strategy

### falcon-wasm Crate

New crate at `falcon-wasm/` wrapping falcon-rs with `wasm-bindgen`:

```rust
#[wasm_bindgen]
pub fn generate_keypair() -> JsValue { ... }
#[wasm_bindgen]
pub fn sign(message: &[u8], secret_key: &[u8]) -> JsValue { ... }
#[wasm_bindgen]
pub fn create_verification_hint(s1: &[u8], pk_ntt: &[u8]) -> JsValue { ... }
#[wasm_bindgen]
pub fn pack_public_key(pk: &[u8]) -> JsValue { ... }
```

### Build

```bash
wasm-pack build falcon-wasm --target web --out-dir ../apps/demo/public/wasm
```

### Loading

WASM loaded from `public/wasm/` via `fetch` + `WebAssembly.instantiate` inside the `WasmRuntime` Effect service. `wasmStatusAtom` tracks loading state. Components show skeleton while loading.

## Super-Ralph Workflow

Workflow directory: `apps/demo/.workflow/`

### Focuses

```typescript
export const focuses = [
  { id: "landing", name: "Landing page (Hero, Why PQ, Stats)" },
  { id: "effect-services", name: "Effect services (FalconService, StarknetService, WasmRuntime)" },
  { id: "wasm", name: "falcon-wasm Rust crate (wasm-bindgen, wasm-pack)" },
  { id: "verify-playground", name: "Verification Playground (interactive demo)" },
  { id: "pipeline-viz", name: "Pipeline Visualizer (animated step-through)" },
  { id: "account-deploy", name: "Account Deploy Flow (mainnet)" },
  { id: "atoms-state", name: "Effect Atoms (reactive state management)" },
  { id: "styling", name: "Styling and design system (Tailwind, components)" },
] as const
```

### Dependency Graph

```
wasm ──────────┐
               ├──→ effect-services ──→ verify-playground
styling ───────┤                   ──→ pipeline-viz
               │                   ──→ account-deploy
landing ───────┘ (independent)
atoms-state ──→ (all client components)
```

## Decisions

| Decision | Choice | Rationale |
|----------|--------|-----------|
| Framework | Next.js 15 App Router | RSC for landing, Effect integration via @prb/effect-next |
| Crypto runtime | Browser WASM | No backend needed, falcon-rs already exists |
| Network | Starknet Mainnet | Maximum credibility for demo |
| State | Effect Atoms | Integrates with Effect services, reactive |
| Styling | Tailwind CSS | Clean technical aesthetic, dark/light toggle |
| Build tool | super-ralph | Parallel ticket-driven development |

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /home/felt/.claude/plugins/cache/claude-plugins-official/superpowers/4.3.1/skills/writing-plans

# Writing Plans

## Overview

Write comprehensive implementation plans assuming the engineer has zero context for our codebase and questionable taste. Document everything they need to know: which files to touch for each task, code, testing, docs they might need to check, how to test it. Give them the whole plan as bite-sized tasks. DRY. YAGNI. TDD. Frequent commits.

Assume they are a skilled developer, but know almost nothing about our toolset or problem domain. Assume they don't know good test design very well.

**Announce at start:** "I'm using the writing-plans skill to create the implementation plan."

**Context:** This should be run in a dedicated worktree (created by brainstorming skill).

**Save plans to:** `docs/plans/YYYY-MM-DD-<feature-name>.md`

## Bite-Sized Task Granularity

**Each step is one action (2-5 minutes):**
- "Write the failing test" - step
- "Run it to make sure it fails" - step
- "Implement the minimal code to make the test pass" - step
- "Run the tests and make sure they pass" - step
- "Commit" - step

## Plan Document Header

**Every plan MUST start with this header:**

```markdown
# [Feature Name] Implementation Plan

> **For Claude:** REQUIRED SUB-SKILL: Use superpowers:executing-plans to implement this plan task-by-task.

**Goal:** [One sentence describing what this builds]

**Architecture:** [2-3 sentences about approach]

**Tech Stack:** [Key technologies/libraries]

---
```

## Task Structure

````markdown
### Task N: [Component Name]

**Files:**
- Create: `exact/path/to/file.py`
- Modify: `exact/path/to/existing.py:123-145`
- Test: `tests/exact/path/to/test.py`

**Step 1: Write the failing test**

```python
def test_specific_behavior():
    result = function(input)
    assert result == expected
```

**Step 2: Run test to verify it fails**

Run: `pytest tests/path/test.py::test_name -v`
Expected: FAIL with "function not defined"

**Step 3: Write minimal implementation**

```python
def function(input):
    return expected
```

**Step 4: Run test to verify it passes**

Run: `pytest tests/path/test.py::test_name -v`
Expected: PASS

**Step 5: Commit**

```bash
git add tests/path/test.py src/path/file.py
git commit -m "feat: add specific feature"
```
````

## Remember
- Exact file paths always
- Complete code in plan (not "add validation")
- Exact commands with expected output
- Reference relevant skills with @ syntax
- DRY, YAGNI, TDD, frequent commits

## Execution Handoff

After saving the plan, offer execution choice:

**"Plan complete and saved to `docs/plans/<filename>.md`. Two execution options:**

**1. Subagent-Driven (this session)** - I dispatch fresh subagent per task, review between tasks, fast iteration

**2. Parallel Session (separate)** - Open new session with executing-plans, batch execution with checkpoints

**Which approach?"**

**If Subagent-Driven chosen:**
- **REQUIRED SUB-SKILL:** Use superpowers:subagent-driven-development
- Stay in this session
- Fresh subagent per task + code review

**If Parallel Session chosen:**
- Guide them to open new session in worktree
- **REQUIRED SUB-SKILL:** New session uses superpowers:executing-plans

I'm using the writing-plans skill to create the implementation plan.

Here's what I found about the **@effect-atom/atom-react** npm package:

## Overview
The latest version is 0.5.0, last published 9 days ago. You can install it by running `npm i @effect-atom/atom-react`.

## What It Is
@effect-atom/atom-react is a reactive toolkit for Effect. The effect-rx package was renamed to effect-atom and can now be installed from npm using @effect-atom/atom-react.

## Key Features
It's a reactive state management library for Effect. The package provides hooks like `useAtomValue`, `useAtomSet`, and `useAtom` for working with reactive state in React components. Atom is a reactive state container.

## Maintenance & Adoption
The npm package receives a total of 9,025 weekly downloads and is classified as popular. There are 5 other projects in the npm registry using @effect-atom/atom-react.

You can find the source code on [GitHub](https://github.com/tim-smart/effect-atom) and detailed documentation with examples at the [Effect Atom Examples site](https://atom.kitlangton.com/).

Links: [{"title":"vite-plugin-wasm - npm","url":"https://www.npmjs.com/package/vite-plugin-wasm"},{"title":"Vite and wasm-pack · vitejs/vite · Discussion #2584","url":"https://github.com/vitejs/vite/discussions/2584"},{"title":"GitHub - Menci/vite-plugin-wasm: Add WebAssembly ESM integration (aka. Webpack's `asyncWebAssembly`) to Vite and support `wasm-pack` generated modules.","url":"https://github.com/Menci/vite-plugin-wasm"},{"title":"Issues Loading and Testing WebAssembly Modules in `vitest` · vitest-dev/vitest · Discussion #4283","url":"https://github.com/vitest-dev/vitest/discussions/4283"},{"title":"Wasm (rust), vite, and pnpm workspace | by Agnislav Onufriichuk | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@agnislav/wasm-rust-vite-and-pnpm-workspace-db561f77c5ca"},{"title":"Loading .wasm files as assets with Turbopack · vercel/next.js · Discussion #75430","url":"https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/75430"},{"title":"Loading WASM in web worker works in dev, but fails in build worker and works in build main.ts · vitejs/vite · Discussion #19111","url":"https://github.com/vitejs/vite/discussions/19111"},{"title":"Rust WebAssembly module in an ES module wrapper from wasm-pack fails to load in Next.js · Issue #29362 · vercel/next.js","url":"https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/29362"},{"title":"vite-plugin-wasm-pack - npm","url":"https://www.npmjs.com/package/vite-plugin-wasm-pack"},{"title":"How to set Webpack 5 in Next.js to use a WASM library compiled for Node.js? · vercel/next.js · Discussion #35637","url":"https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/35637"}]

Based on the search results, here's information about WASM loading patterns with wasm-pack, vite-plugin-wasm, and Next.js 15:

## Key Information Found

### vite-plugin-wasm Support
The vite-plugin-wasm plugin supports Vite 2.x to 7.x, making it compatible with modern Vite setups. This plugin adds WebAssembly ESM integration (similar to Webpack's asyncWebAssembly) to Vite and supports wasm-pack generated modules.

### Configuration Pattern
The typical configuration includes both wasm and vite-plugin-top-level-await plugins, with the latter needed unless you target very modern browsers (set build.target to esnext). A basic setup looks like:

```javascript
import wasm from "vite-plugin-wasm";
import topLevelAwait from "vite-plugin-top-level-await";

export default defineConfig({
  plugins: [wasm(), topLevelAwait()]
});
```

### Next.js 15 Considerations
Both copying .wasm files to public/ and using API routes play nicely with Next 15 + Turbopack. Since Next.js 15 uses Turbopack by default, there are specific patterns recommended: The fastest migration is to copy the .wasm into public/ at install/build and keep loader code using WebAssembly.instantiateStreaming with a fallback, or use an API route to serve the package file directly—both play nicely with Next 15 + Turbopack.

### Practical Vite Configuration
A practical Vite configuration sets the plugins with wasm(), builds with target 'esnext', and uses format 'es' for compatibility with modern WASM loading patterns.

content.content:
1→#!/usr/bin/env python3
     2→# /// script
     3→# requires-python = ">=3.11"
     4→# dependencies = []
     5→# ///
     6→"""
     7→Super-Ralph workflow initializer.
     8→
     9→Usage:
    10→  uv run scripts/init_super_ralph.py <target-dir> --root <repo-root> [OPTIONS]
    11→
    12→Sets up a super-ralph workflow template in <target-dir> that drives
    13→ticket-based development against <repo-root>.
    14→
    15→Examples:
    16→  uv run scripts/init_super_ralph.py ./scripts/workflow --root ../..
    17→  uv run scripts/init_super_ralph.py ./workflow --root . --name "My Project" --id my-project
    18→  uv run scripts/init_super_ralph.py ./workflow --root ../.. --no-install
    19→"""
    20→
    21→import argparse
    22→import os
    23→import shutil
    24→import subprocess
    25→import sys
    26→from pathlib import Path
    27→
    28→TEMPLATE_DIR = Path(__file__).parent.parent / "template"
    29→
    30→
    31→def parse_args() -> argparse.Namespace:
    32→    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(
    33→        description="Initialize a super-ralph workflow.",
    34→        formatter_class=argparse.RawDescriptionHelpFormatter,
    35→    )
    36→    parser.add_argument("target", help="Directory to create the workflow in")
    37→    parser.add_argument(
    38→        "--root",
    39→        required=True,
    40→        help="Relative path from <target> to the git repository root (e.g. '../..')",
    41→    )
    42→    parser.add_argument("--name", help="Human-readable project name", default=None)
    43→    parser.add_argument("--id", help="Kebab-case project ID", default=None)
    44→    parser.add_argument(
    45→        "--no-install", action="store_true", help="Skip bun install"
    46→    )
    47→    parser.add_argument(
    48→        "--no-jj", action="store_true", help="Skip jj init"
    49→    )
    50→    return parser.parse_args()
    51→
    52→
    53→def copy_template(src: Path, dst: Path) -> None:
    54→    if not src.exists():
    55→        print(f"ERROR: Template dir not found: {src}", file=sys.stderr)
    56→        sys.exit(1)
    57→    dst.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    58→    for item in src.rglob("*"):
    59→        if "node_modules" in item.parts:
    60→            continue
    61→        relative = item.relative_to(src)
    62→        target_path = dst / relative
    63→        if item.is_dir():
    64→            target_path.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    65→        else:
    66→            target_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    67→            shutil.copy2(item, target_path)
    68→    print(f"  Copied template → {dst}")
    69→
    70→
    71→def compute_url_depth(root_relative: str) -> str:
    72→    """Convert a filesystem relative path to a URL-relative path for import.meta.url.
    73→
    74→    From components/workflow.tsx we need one extra '../' because the file is
    75→    inside a components/ subdirectory of the workflow dir.
    76→    """
    77→    # Normalise: e.g. "../.." → 2 levels up from workflow dir
    78→    parts = Path(root_relative).parts
    79→    ups = sum(1 for p in parts if p == "..")
    80→    # +1 because workflow.tsx is inside components/
    81→    return "/".join([".."] * (ups + 1))
    82→
    83→
    84→def patch_placeholders(target: Path, project_id: str, project_name: str, root_relative: str) -> None:
    85→    """Replace {{PLACEHOLDER}} tokens in all text files."""
    86→    url_depth = compute_url_depth(root_relative)
    87→    replacements = {
    88→        "{{PROJECT_ID}}": project_id,
    89→        "{{PROJECT_NAME}}": project_name,
    90→        "{{ROOT_RELATIVE}}": root_relative,
    91→        "{{ROOT_URL_DEPTH}}": url_depth,
    92→    }
    93→    for fpath in target.rglob("*"):
    94→        if fpath.is_dir() or "node_modules" in fpath.parts:
    95→            continue
    96→        # Only patch text files
    97→        if fpath.suffix not in (".ts", ".tsx", ".json", ".toml", ".md"):
    98→            continue
    99→        try:
   100→            content = fpath.read_text()
   101→        except UnicodeDecodeError:
   102→            continue
   103→        original = content
   104→        for placeholder, value in replacements.items():
   105→            content = content.replace(placeholder, value)
   106→        if content != original:
   107→            fpath.write_text(content)
   108→
   109→
   110→def find_repo_root(start: Path) -> Path | None:
   111→    current = start.resolve()
   112→    while True:
   113→        if (current / ".git").exists():
   114→            return current
   115→        parent = current.parent
   116→        if parent == current:
   117→            return None
   118→        current = parent
   119→
   120→
   121→def init_jj(target: Path) -> None:
   122→    repo_root = find_repo_root(target)
   123→    if repo_root is None:
   124→        print("  No git repo found — skipping jj init")
   125→        return
   126→    if (repo_root / ".jj").exists():
   127→        print(f"  jj already initialized at {repo_root}")
   128→        return
   129→    if not shutil.which("jj"):
   130→        print("  jj not on PATH — install: brew install jj")
   131→        return
   132→    print(f"  Running jj git init --colocate in {repo_root}...")
   133→    subprocess.run(["jj", "git", "init", "--colocate"], cwd=repo_root, capture_output=False)
   134→
   135→
   136→def run_bun_install(target: Path) -> None:
   137→    print("  Running bun install...")
   138→    result = subprocess.run(["bun", "install"], cwd=target, capture_output=False)
   139→    if result.returncode != 0:
   140→        print("  WARNING: bun install failed. Run it manually.", file=sys.stderr)
   141→
   142→
   143→def main() -> None:
   144→    args = parse_args()
   145→
   146→    target = Path(args.target).resolve()
   147→    project_id = args.id or target.name
   148→    project_name = args.name or project_id.replace("-", " ").title()
   149→    root_relative = args.root
   150→
   151→    print(f"\nInitializing super-ralph workflow:")
   152→    print(f"  Target     : {target}")
   153→    print(f"  Project ID : {project_id}")
   154→    print(f"  Name       : {project_name}")
   155→    print(f"  Repo root  : {root_relative} (from target dir)")
   156→    print()
   157→
   158→    if target.exists() and any(target.iterdir()):
   159→        answer = input(f"  {target} already exists. Overwrite? [y/N] ").strip().lower()
   160→        if answer != "y":
   161→            print("  Aborted.")
   162→            sys.exit(0)
   163→
   164→    copy_template(TEMPLATE_DIR, target)
   165→    patch_placeholders(target, project_id, project_name, root_relative)
   166→
   167→    if not args.no_jj:
   168→        init_jj(target)
   169→
   170→    if not args.no_install:
   171→        if shutil.which("bun"):
   172→            run_bun_install(target)
   173→        else:
   174→            print("  bun not on PATH — run `bun install` manually.")
   175→
   176→    # Display path
   177→    try:
   178→        rel = target.relative_to(Path.cwd())
   179→    except ValueError:
   180→        rel = target
   181→
   182→    print(f"""
   183→Done! Next steps:
   184→
   185→  1. Edit focuses:
   186→       {rel}/components/focuses.ts
   187→     Replace the placeholder focuses with your project's actual domain areas.
   188→
   189→  2. Customize the workflow:
   190→       {rel}/components/workflow.tsx
   191→     Fill in the TODO sections:
   192→       - Agent system prompts (planning, implementation, testing, reviewing)
   193→       - specsPath, referenceFiles
   194→       - buildCmds, testCmds
   195→       - codeStyle, reviewChecklist
   196→       - Agent models and timeouts
   197→
   198→  3. Run:
   199→       cd {rel} && bun run index.ts
   200→""")
   201→
   202→
   203→if __name__ == "__main__":
   204→    main()

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Starknet.js account deploy API:

## Account Deploy API Overview

The Account API introduces new methods that allow Accounts to create and verify signatures with a custom Signer, declare and deploy Contracts and new Accounts. This API is the primary way to interact with an account contract on Starknet.

## Practical Implementation

The deployContract method is used with an Account instance, passing in a classHash parameter to deploy a contract, followed by waiting for the transaction to be confirmed via provider.waitForTransaction().

## Recent Context (2025-2026)

A detailed post-mortem of the January 5, 2026 Starknet outage was documented, explaining the blockifier bug, reorg, recovery process, and steps taken to prevent future incidents. This suggests that the API has been actively maintained and tested through recent network events.

For the most current and detailed API documentation, you can visit the official Starknet.js documentation at https://starknetjs.com/docs/API/, which provides comprehensive guides on using the Account API for deployments and other interactions with the Starknet network.

content.content:
1→# Super-Ralph Customization Guide
     2→
     3→## SuperRalph Props Reference
     4→
     5→All props are passed to `<SuperRalph>` in `components/workflow.tsx`.
     6→
     7→### Required Props
     8→
     9→| Prop | Type | Description |
    10→|------|------|-------------|
    11→| `ctx` | `SmithersCtx` | Runtime context (from `smithers()` wrapper) |
    12→| `outputs` | `RalphOutputs` | Schema references (from `createSmithers`) |
    13→| `focuses` | `Array<{id, name}>` | Domain focus areas for discovery/review |
    14→| `projectId` | `string` | Kebab-case identifier |
    15→| `projectName` | `string` | Human-readable name |
    16→| `specsPath` | `string` | Path to spec docs (relative to repo root) |
    17→| `referenceFiles` | `string[]` | Reference doc/directory paths |
    18→| `buildCmds` | `Record<string, string>` | Named build commands that must pass |
    19→| `testCmds` | `Record<string, string>` | Named test commands that must pass |
    20→| `codeStyle` | `string` | Code style description for reviewers |
    21→| `reviewChecklist` | `string[]` | Review checklist items |
    22→| `maxConcurrency` | `number` | Max parallel agent tasks |
    23→| `agents` | `AgentConfig` | Per-role agent configuration |
    24→
    25→### Optional Props
    26→
    27→| Prop | Type | Default | Description |
    28→|------|------|---------|-------------|
    29→| `taskRetries` | `number` | `3` | Per-task retry budget |
    30→| `progressFile` | `string` | `"PROGRESS.md"` | Progress summary file |
    31→| `findingsFile` | `string` | `"docs/test-suite-findings.md"` | Integration test findings |
    32→| `skipPhases` | `Set<string>` | `new Set()` | Phases to skip on iteration 0 |
    33→| `focusTestSuites` | `Record<FocusId, {...}>` | — | Per-focus test suite hints |
    34→| `focusDirs` | `Record<FocusId, string[]>` | — | Per-focus directory hints for reviewers |
    35→| `preLandChecks` | `string[]` | — | Fast checks run in worktree before landing |
    36→| `postLandChecks` | `string[]` | — | Slow checks run after merge queue rebase |
    37→| `maxSpeculativeDepth` | `number` | `3` | Merge queue speculation depth |
    38→
    39→## Agent Configuration
    40→
    41→### Agent Types
    42→
    43→```ts
    44→import { ClaudeCodeAgent, CodexAgent, GeminiAgent, KimiAgent } from "smithers-orchestrator";
    45→```
    46→
    47→| Agent | CLI | Key Options |
    48→|-------|-----|-------------|
    49→| `ClaudeCodeAgent` | `claude` | `model`, `systemPrompt`, `cwd`, `dangerouslySkipPermissions`, `timeoutMs` |
    50→| `CodexAgent` | `codex` | `model`, `systemPrompt`, `cwd`, `yolo`, `config: { model_reasoning_effort }`, `timeoutMs` |
    51→| `GeminiAgent` | `gemini` | `model`, `systemPrompt`, `cwd`, `yolo`, `timeoutMs` |
    52→| `KimiAgent` | `kimi` | `model`, `systemPrompt`, `cwd`, `yolo`, `thinking`, `timeoutMs` |
    53→
    54→### Fallback Chains
    55→
    56→Pass agent arrays for rate-limit resilience. Attempt 1 uses `agents[0]`, attempt 2+ uses the next:
    57→
    58→```ts
    59→agents: {
    60→  planning: [
    61→    new GeminiAgent({ model: "gemini-2.5-pro", ... }),
    62→    new ClaudeCodeAgent({ model: "claude-opus-4-6", ... }),
    63→  ],
    64→}
    65→```
    66→
    67→### Role Mapping
    68→
    69→| Role | Steps Using It |
    70→|------|---------------|
    71→| `planning` | Research, Plan, Discover |
    72→| `implementation` | Implement, ReviewFix |
    73→| `testing` | Test, BuildVerify, IntegrationTest |
    74→| `reviewing` | SpecReview, CodeReview, CategoryReview |
    75→| `reporting` | Report, UpdateProgress |
    76→| `mergeQueue` | Land (optional, has built-in default) |
    77→
    78→## Pipeline Per Ticket
    79→
    80→Each ticket goes through this pipeline (inside a jj worktree):
    81→
    82→```
    83→Research → Plan → [Implement → Test → BuildVerify → (SpecReview ∥ CodeReview) → ReviewFix?] → Report → MergeQueue
    84→                  └──────────────────── ValidationLoop (reruns if review finds issues) ──────┘
    85→```
    86→
    87→## Focus Areas Design
    88→
    89→Good focuses have clear boundaries and match your project's architecture:
    90→
    91→```ts
    92→// Good: maps to distinct code areas, agent knows where to look
    93→{ id: "auth", name: "Authentication (JWT, OAuth, session management)" },
    94→{ id: "api",  name: "REST API routes and middleware" },
    95→
    96→// Bad: too broad, agents generate unfocused tickets
    97→{ id: "code", name: "All code" },
    98→```
    99→
   100→Remove focuses when they reach production quality — saves agent cycles.
   101→
   102→## Phase Skipping
   103→
   104→Skip phases on the first iteration via env var:
   105→
   106→```bash
   107→SKIPTO_PHASE=TICKETS bun run index.ts    # Skip progress/codebase-review/discover
   108→SKIPTO_PHASE=DISCOVER bun run index.ts   # Skip only progress and codebase-review
   109→```
   110→
   111→## focusTestSuites and focusDirs
   112→
   113→Optional per-focus hints that help agents know what to test/review:
   114→
   115→```ts
   116→export const focusTestSuites: Record<FocusId, { suites: string[]; setupHints: string; testDirs: string[] }> = {
   117→  auth: {
   118→    suites: ["pnpm test -- --grep auth"],
   119→    setupHints: "Requires TEST_JWT_SECRET env var",
   120→    testDirs: ["test/auth/"],
   121→  },
   122→};
   123→
   124→export const focusDirs: Record<FocusId, string[]> = {
   125→  auth: ["src/auth/", "src/middleware/auth.ts"],
   126→};
   127→```
   128→
   129→## Custom Step Overrides
   130→
   131→Override any pipeline step with a custom component via props:
   132→
   133→```tsx
   134→<SuperRalph
   135→  {...baseProps}
   136→  codeReview={
   137→    <SuperRalph.CodeReview
   138→      agent={primaryAgent}
   139→      additionalAgents={[
   140→        { agent: secondAgent, outputKey: "code_review_second" },
   141→      ]}
   142→      reviewChecklist={checklist}
   143→    />
   144→  }
   145→/>
   146→```
   147→
   148→Available override props: `updateProgress`, `discover`, `integrationTest`, `categoryReview`,
   149→`research`, `plan`, `implement`, `test`, `buildVerify`, `specReview`, `codeReview`,
   150→`reviewFix`, `report`, `land`.
   151→

2. Read ~/.claude/plugins/cache/eni-skills/personal-skills/1.0.0/skills/super-ralph/references/customization.md

content.content:
1→import { ClaudeCodeAgent, CodexAgent } from "smithers-orchestrator";
     2→import { SuperRalph } from "super-ralph";
     3→import { WORKFLOW_MAX_CONCURRENCY, WORKFLOW_TASK_RETRIES } from "../config";
     4→import { outputs, smithers, Workflow } from "../smithers";
     5→import { focuses } from "./focuses";
     6→
     7→// TODO: Adjust URL depth so REPO_ROOT resolves to the git repository root.
     8→//   From <repo>/workflow/components/  → use ("../..")
     9→//   From <repo>/scripts/wf/components/ → use ("../../..")
    10→const REPO_ROOT = new URL("{{ROOT_URL_DEPTH}}", import.meta.url).pathname.replace(
    11→	/\/$/,
    12→	"",
    13→);
    14→
    15→// ─── Agent system prompts ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    16→// TODO: Replace these with project-specific instructions.
    17→// Each prompt is injected as the systemPrompt for agents in that role.
    18→
    19→const PLANNING_PROMPT = `You are planning work for {{PROJECT_NAME}}.
    20→
    21→TODO: Add project-specific planning instructions here:
    22→- Architecture rules and constraints
    23→- Priority ordering for features
    24→- Spec/PRD files the agent MUST read before planning
    25→- Security invariants to maintain`;
    26→
    27→const IMPLEMENTATION_PROMPT = `You are implementing features for {{PROJECT_NAME}}.
    28→
    29→TODO: Add project-specific implementation instructions:
    30→- Mandatory patterns (frameworks, error handling, etc.)
    31→- Forbidden patterns (things to never do)
    32→- TDD mandate: write tests FIRST
    33→- Build/typecheck commands to run after changes`;
    34→
    35→const TESTING_PROMPT = `You are testing {{PROJECT_NAME}}.
    36→
    37→TODO: Add project-specific testing instructions:
    38→- Test runner command
    39→- Testing priorities and frameworks
    40→- Mock/fixture patterns
    41→- Coverage requirements`;
    42→
    43→const REVIEWING_PROMPT = `You are reviewing code for {{PROJECT_NAME}}.
    44→
    45→TODO: Add project-specific review instructions:
    46→- Review checklist (same as reviewChecklist prop below)
    47→- Severity rules: what makes an issue major vs critical
    48→- Spec/PRD to check compliance against`;
    49→
    50→const REPORTING_PROMPT = `Write completion reports for {{PROJECT_NAME}}.
    51→Be concise. Report what was done, what tests pass, what remains.`;
    52→
    53→// ─── Phase skipping ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    54→
    55→type Phase = "PROGRESS" | "CODEBASE_REVIEW" | "DISCOVER" | "TICKETS" | "INTEGRATION_TEST";
    56→const PHASES: Phase[] = ["PROGRESS", "CODEBASE_REVIEW", "DISCOVER", "TICKETS", "INTEGRATION_TEST"];
    57→const SKIPTO_PHASE_RAW = process.env.SKIPTO_PHASE?.trim() ?? "";
    58→const SKIPTO_PHASE =
    59→	SKIPTO_PHASE_RAW === ""
    60→		? null
    61→		: PHASES.includes(SKIPTO_PHASE_RAW as Phase)
    62→			? (SKIPTO_PHASE_RAW as Phase)
    63→			: null;
    64→
    65→if (SKIPTO_PHASE_RAW !== "" && SKIPTO_PHASE == null) {
    66→	throw new Error(
    67→		`invalid SKIPTO_PHASE: ${SKIPTO_PHASE_RAW}. Expected one of: ${PHASES.join(", ")}`,
    68→	);
    69→}
    70→
    71→// ─── Workflow ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    72→
    73→export default smithers((ctx) => {
    74→	const skipPhases = new Set<string>();
    75→	if (SKIPTO_PHASE && ctx.iteration === 0) {
    76→		const skipIndex = PHASES.indexOf(SKIPTO_PHASE);
    77→		PHASES.forEach((phase, i) => {
    78→			if (i < skipIndex) skipPhases.add(phase);
    79→		});
    80→	}
    81→
    82→	return (
    83→		<Workflow name="{{PROJECT_ID}}-factory">
    84→			<SuperRalph
    85→				ctx={ctx}
    86→				outputs={outputs}
    87→				focuses={focuses}
    88→				projectId="{{PROJECT_ID}}"
    89→				projectName="{{PROJECT_NAME}}"
    90→				// ── TODO: Customize these props ───────────────────────────────────
    91→				specsPath="docs/specs/"
    92→				referenceFiles={[
    93→					// TODO: Paths (relative to repo root) that agents should read for context.
    94→					// PRDs, reference implementations, existing source, etc.
    95→					"README.md",
    96→				]}
    97→				buildCmds={{
    98→					// TODO: Commands that must pass for a ticket to be "build-verified".
    99→					typecheck: "bun run typecheck",
   100→					build: "bun run build",
   101→				}}
   102→				testCmds={{
   103→					// TODO: Commands that must pass for a ticket to be "test-verified".
   104→					unit: "bun test",
   105→				}}
   106→				codeStyle={[
   107→					// TODO: Code style rules agents must follow.
   108→					"TypeScript, strict mode",
   109→				].join("\n")}
   110→				reviewChecklist={[
   111→					// TODO: Checklist items for code/spec reviewers.
   112→					// Agents mark severity=major/critical when these are violated.
   113→					"Spec compliance — does implementation match the spec?",
   114→					"Test coverage — every public API has tests",
   115→					"Security — no injection, no leaked secrets",
   116→				]}
   117→				// ── Agent configuration ──────────────────────────────────────────
   118→				maxConcurrency={WORKFLOW_MAX_CONCURRENCY}
   119→				taskRetries={WORKFLOW_TASK_RETRIES}
   120→				skipPhases={skipPhases}
   121→				agents={{
   122→					// TODO: Adjust models and timeouts per role.
   123→					// Use agent arrays for fallback: [primary, fallback1, fallback2]
   124→					// Available: ClaudeCodeAgent, CodexAgent, GeminiAgent, KimiAgent
   125→					planning: new ClaudeCodeAgent({
   126→						model: "claude-opus-4-6",
   127→						systemPrompt: PLANNING_PROMPT,
   128→						cwd: REPO_ROOT,
   129→						dangerouslySkipPermissions: true,
   130→						timeoutMs: 30 * 60 * 1000,
   131→					}),
   132→					implementation: new CodexAgent({
   133→						model: "gpt-5.3-codex",
   134→						systemPrompt: IMPLEMENTATION_PROMPT,
   135→						config: {
   136→							model_reasoning_effort: "high",
   137→						},
   138→						cwd: REPO_ROOT,
   139→						yolo: true,
   140→						timeoutMs: 60 * 60 * 1000,
   141→					}),
   142→					testing: new ClaudeCodeAgent({
   143→						model: "claude-sonnet-4-6",
   144→						systemPrompt: TESTING_PROMPT,
   145→						cwd: REPO_ROOT,
   146→						dangerouslySkipPermissions: true,
   147→						timeoutMs: 30 * 60 * 1000,
   148→					}),
   149→					reviewing: new ClaudeCodeAgent({
   150→						model: "claude-opus-4-6",
   151→						systemPrompt: REVIEWING_PROMPT,
   152→						cwd: REPO_ROOT,
   153→						dangerouslySkipPermissions: true,
   154→						timeoutMs: 20 * 60 * 1000,
   155→					}),
   156→					reporting: new ClaudeCodeAgent({
   157→						model: "claude-sonnet-4-6",
   158→						systemPrompt: REPORTING_PROMPT,
   159→						cwd: REPO_ROOT,
   160→						dangerouslySkipPermissions: true,
   161→						timeoutMs: 10 * 60 * 1000,
   162→					}),
   163→				}}
   164→			/>
   165→		</Workflow>
   166→	);
   167→});
   168→

content.content:
1→/**
     2→ * Focus areas for the workflow.
     3→ * TODO: Replace with 6-15 focus areas that cover your project's domains.
     4→ *
     5→ * Each focus becomes:
     6→ * - A CategoryReview audit (one per loop iteration)
     7→ * - An IntegrationTest suite (one per loop iteration)
     8→ * - A bucket for ticket discovery to generate work in
     9→ *
    10→ * Guidelines:
    11→ * - id: kebab-case unique identifier
    12→ * - name: human-readable description (agents see this in prompts)
    13→ * - Remove focuses once they reach production quality (saves agent time)
    14→ */
    15→export const focuses = [

Based on the search results, I found information about the `deployAccount` method in starknet.js:

## deployAccount Method API Documentation

The `deployAccount` method is used to deploy an account contract on Starknet. Here's what you need to know about it:

### Method Signature

The method accepts a payload object with parameters including `classHash`, `constructorCalldata`, `addressSalt`, and returns an object containing `transaction_hash` and `contract_address`.

### Example Usage

```javascript
const OZaccount = new Account(provider, OZcontractAddress, privateKey);
const { transaction_hash, contract_address } = await OZaccount.deployAccount({
  classHash: OZaccountClassHash,
  constructorCalldata: OZaccountConstructorCallData,
  addressSalt: starkKeyPub,
});
```

### Key Points

Account contracts on Starknet cannot be deployed without paying a fee, so you'll need to ensure your account has sufficient funds before calling this method.

For the most current and detailed API documentation, you can visit the official Starknet.js documentation at https://starknetjs.com/docs/API/ or the specific Account class documentation.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Next.js 15, webpack WASM, and asyncWebAssembly configuration:

## Key Findings:

Since webpack 5, WebAssembly is not enabled by default and is flagged as an experimental feature. You need to enable one of the WebAssembly experiments via 'experiments.asyncWebAssembly: true' (based on async modules) or 'experiments.syncWebAssembly: true' (like webpack 4, deprecated).

## Configuration Pattern:

The standard way to enable asyncWebAssembly in Next.js is through the webpack configuration in `next.config.js`:

The configuration involves setting `config.experiments = { asyncWebAssembly: true, layers: true }` in the webpack function.

asyncWebAssembly supports the new WebAssembly according to the updated specification, making a WebAssembly module an async module.

## Additional Context:

When experimental features are enabled, Next.js warns that experimental features are not covered by semver and may cause unexpected or broken application behavior, and should be used at your own risk.

The search results primarily contain examples from Next.js versions 12-14, with some references to Next.js 15 in documentation. If you need specific information about Next.js 15's handling of asyncWebAssembly or any experimental changes in that version, you may want to check the official Next.js 15 documentation or release notes directly.

content.content:
1→//! WASM bindings for Falcon-512 signature scheme.
     2→//!
     3→//! This module provides JavaScript-friendly bindings for browser use.
     4→//! Currently uses SHAKE256 hash; Poseidon support will be added for Starknet.
     5→
     6→#![cfg(feature = "wasm")]
     7→
     8→use wasm_bindgen::prelude::*;
     9→
    10→use crate::falcon::{Falcon, Signature, VerifyingKey, PUBLIC_KEY_LEN};
    11→use crate::hash_to_point::Shake256Hash;
    12→use crate::SALT_LEN;
    13→
    14→/// Generate a new Falcon-512 keypair.
    15→///
    16→/// Returns a JavaScript object with `sk` (secret key bytes) and `vk` (verifying key bytes).
    17→///
    18→/// Note: Key generation is slow (~1-2 minutes) due to NTRU complexity.
    19→#[wasm_bindgen]
    20→pub fn keygen(seed: &[u8]) -> Result<JsValue, JsError> {
    21→    if seed.len() < 32 {
    22→        return Err(JsError::new("Seed must be at least 32 bytes"));
    23→    }
    24→
    25→    let (sk, vk) = Falcon::<Shake256Hash>::keygen_with_seed(seed);
    26→
    27→    let result = js_sys::Object::new();
    28→    js_sys::Reflect::set(
    29→        &result,
    30→        &"sk".into(),
    31→        &js_sys::Uint8Array::from(&sk.to_bytes()[..]),
    32→    )
    33→    .map_err(|e| JsError::new(&format!("Failed to set sk: {:?}", e)))?;
    34→    js_sys::Reflect::set(
    35→        &result,
    36→        &"vk".into(),
    37→        &js_sys::Uint8Array::from(&vk.to_bytes()[..]),
    38→    )
    39→    .map_err(|e| JsError::new(&format!("Failed to set vk: {:?}", e)))?;
    40→
    41→    Ok(result.into())
    42→}
    43→
    44→/// Sign a message with a secret key.
    45→///
    46→/// Note: This function is a placeholder. Full signing requires the secret key
    47→/// structure which is not easily serializable to JS. Use the native Rust API
    48→/// for signing, or implement a custom serialization format.
    49→#[wasm_bindgen]
    50→pub fn sign(_sk_bytes: &[u8], _message: &[u8], _seed: &[u8]) -> Result<Vec<u8>, JsError> {
    51→    Err(JsError::new(
    52→        "sign() not yet implemented for WASM. Use native Rust API.",
    53→    ))
    54→}
    55→
    56→/// Verify a signature.
    57→///
    58→/// Parameters:
    59→/// - `vk_bytes`: The verifying key (public key) bytes
    60→/// - `message`: The signed message
    61→/// - `signature`: The signature bytes
    62→///
    63→/// Returns true if the signature is valid, false otherwise.
    64→#[wasm_bindgen]
    65→pub fn verify(vk_bytes: &[u8], message: &[u8], signature: &[u8]) -> Result<bool, JsError> {
    66→    if vk_bytes.len() != PUBLIC_KEY_LEN {
    67→        return Err(JsError::new(&format!(
    68→            "Invalid verifying key length: expected {}, got {}",
    69→            PUBLIC_KEY_LEN,
    70→            vk_bytes.len()
    71→        )));
    72→    }
    73→
    74→    let vk_arr: [u8; PUBLIC_KEY_LEN] = vk_bytes
    75→        .try_into()
    76→        .map_err(|_| JsError::new("Invalid verifying key"))?;
    77→
    78→    let vk = VerifyingKey::from_bytes(&vk_arr).map_err(|e| JsError::new(&e.to_string()))?;
    79→
    80→    let sig = Signature::from_bytes(signature).map_err(|e| JsError::new(&e.to_string()))?;
    81→
    82→    Falcon::<Shake256Hash>::verify(&vk, message, &sig).map_err(|e| JsError::new(&e.to_string()))
    83→}
    84→
    85→/// Get the public key length in bytes.
    86→#[wasm_bindgen]
    87→pub fn public_key_length() -> usize {
    88→    PUBLIC_KEY_LEN
    89→}
    90→
    91→/// Get the salt length in bytes.
    92→#[wasm_bindgen]
    93→pub fn salt_length() -> usize {
    94→    SALT_LEN
    95→}
    96→

Based on the search results, I found information about the `effect-nextjs` package by mcrovero:

Effect-nextjs provides typed helpers for building Next.js App Router pages, layouts, server components, and actions with the Effect library.

## Key Features:

1. **Type-safe Route Parameters**: The package includes PageProps and LayoutProps types that provide fully typed route parameters, such as slug parameters in routes like `/blog/[slug]`.

2. **Layout Support with Parallel Routes**: LayoutProps supports fully typed parallel route slots in layouts.

3. **Navigation Utilities**: The package provides Effect versions of Next.js navigation utilities including Redirect, PermanentRedirect, and NotFound.

4. **Middleware Support**: Effect-nextjs includes a NextMiddleware system that allows you to provide typed context (like CurrentUser) to pages and handle authentication through middleware.

5. **Effect Integration**: The package uses Effect.fn to get automatic telemetry spans for page loads and better stack traces.

The package is available on npm as `@mcrovero/effect-nextjs` and can be found on [GitHub at github.com/mcrovero/effect-nextjs](https://github.com/mcrovero/effect-nextjs).


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about starknet.js v9 Account `deployAccount` functionality:

## Overview

The `deployAccount` method accepts parameters including `classHash`, `constructorCalldata`, `addressSalt`, and `contractAddress`, returning a `DeployContractResponse`.

## Key Features

The method handles resource bounds estimation if not provided, and calculates the contract address from the hash.

Starknet.js provides a `declareAndDeploy()` function that performs both contract declaration and deployment in one step, with these operations being methods of the Account object.

## Contract Deployment Process

In Starknet, a new contract must be added in two phases: creating the contract class and then deploying an instance of it, with the contract class needing to be declared before deployment.

## Important Context

In Starknet's account model, unlike Ethereum's externally owned accounts, every account is a contract that forwards messages signed from the account's keypair to invoke specified destination contract addresses.

The search results reference the starknet.js codebase and documentation, showing that `deployAccount` is a core Account method for deploying new account contracts to the Starknet network.

content.text:
Here is the complete breakdown of the Super Ralph init workflow.

---

## Exact Init Command

```bash
uv run ~/.claude/plugins/cache/eni-skills/personal-skills/1.0.0/skills/super-ralph/scripts/init_super_ralph.py <target-dir> --root <repo-root> [OPTIONS]
```

**Concrete example** (workflow dir inside your repo at `./scripts/workflow`, repo root is two levels up from there):

```bash
uv run ~/.claude/plugins/cache/eni-skills/personal-skills/1.0.0/skills/super-ralph/scripts/init_super_ralph.py \
  ./scripts/workflow --root ../..
```

### Optional flags

| Flag | Effect |
|------|--------|
| `--name "My Project"` | Human-readable project name (default: derived from target dir name, title-cased) |
| `--id my-project` | Kebab-case project ID (default: target directory name) |
| `--no-install` | Skip automatic `bun install` |
| `--no-jj` | Skip `jj git init --colocate` |

---

## Files It Creates

The script copies a template directory and performs placeholder substitution. You get these 9 files in `<target-dir>/`:

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `index.ts` | Entry point / run manager. Interactive CLI for starting, resuming, cancelling, and deleting runs. Uses `bun run index.ts` to launch. |
| `smithers.ts` | Creates the Smithers runtime (`createSmithers`) with Ralph output schemas and a SQLite DB at `./<project-id>-build.db`. |
| `config.ts` | Exports `WORKFLOW_MAX_CONCURRENCY` and `WORKFLOW_TASK_RETRIES` (parsed from env vars, defaults 8 and 2). |
| `preload.ts` | Bun preload script that registers the MDX plugin from `smithers-orchestrator`. |
| `bunfig.toml` | Single line: `preload = ["./preload.ts"]` |
| `package.json` | Named `<project-id>-workflow`. Depends on `smithers-orchestrator` and `super-ralph`. |
| `tsconfig.json` | Strict TypeScript config with JSX react-jsx, ESNext target. |
| `components/workflow.tsx` | **The main file you customize.** Contains the `<SuperRalph>` component with all props: agent configs, build/test commands, system prompts, review checklist, etc. |
| `components/focuses.ts` | **Focus areas definition.** Array of `{id, name}` objects representing domain areas for discovery, review, and integration testing. |

### Placeholders substituted in these files

The script replaces four tokens across all `.ts`, `.tsx`, `.json`, `.toml`, and `.md` files:

| Placeholder | Replaced With |
|-------------|---------------|
| `{{PROJECT_ID}}` | Kebab-case ID (e.g. `s2morrow`) |
| `{{PROJECT_NAME}}` | Human-readable name (e.g. `S2morrow`) |
| `{{ROOT_RELATIVE}}` | The `--root` argument verbatim (e.g. `../..`) |
| `{{ROOT_URL_DEPTH}}` | Computed URL-relative path for `import.meta.url` resolution in `workflow.tsx` (adds one extra `../` because `workflow.tsx` is inside `components/`) |

### Side effects beyond file creation

content.text:
Here is the complete breakdown of the Super Ralph init workflow.

---

## Exact Init Command

```bash
uv run ~/.claude/plugins/cache/eni-skills/personal-skills/1.0.0/skills/super-ralph/scripts/init_super_ralph.py <target-dir> --root <repo-root> [OPTIONS]
```

**Concrete example** (workflow dir inside your repo at `./scripts/workflow`, repo root is two levels up from there):

```bash
uv run ~/.claude/plugins/cache/eni-skills/personal-skills/1.0.0/skills/super-ralph/scripts/init_super_ralph.py \
  ./scripts/workflow --root ../..
```

### Optional flags

| Flag | Effect |
|------|--------|
| `--name "My Project"` | Human-readable project name (default: derived from target dir name, title-cased) |
| `--id my-project` | Kebab-case project ID (default: target directory name) |
| `--no-install` | Skip automatic `bun install` |
| `--no-jj` | Skip `jj git init --colocate` |

---

## Files It Creates

The script copies a template directory and performs placeholder substitution. You get these 9 files in `<target-dir>/`:

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `index.ts` | Entry point / run manager. Interactive CLI for starting, resuming, cancelling, and deleting runs. Uses `bun run index.ts` to launch. |
| `smithers.ts` | Creates the Smithers runtime (`createSmithers`) with Ralph output schemas and a SQLite DB at `./<project-id>-build.db`. |
| `config.ts` | Exports `WORKFLOW_MAX_CONCURRENCY` and `WORKFLOW_TASK_RETRIES` (parsed from env vars, defaults 8 and 2). |
| `preload.ts` | Bun preload script that registers the MDX plugin from `smithers-orchestrator`. |
| `bunfig.toml` | Single line: `preload = ["./preload.ts"]` |
| `package.json` | Named `<project-id>-workflow`. Depends on `smithers-orchestrator` and `super-ralph`. |
| `tsconfig.json` | Strict TypeScript config with JSX react-jsx, ESNext target. |
| `components/workflow.tsx` | **The main file you customize.** Contains the `<SuperRalph>` component with all props: agent configs, build/test commands, system prompts, review checklist, etc. |
| `components/focuses.ts` | **Focus areas definition.** Array of `{id, name}` objects representing domain areas for discovery, review, and integration testing. |

### Placeholders substituted in these files

The script replaces four tokens across all `.ts`, `.tsx`, `.json`, `.toml`, and `.md` files:

| Placeholder | Replaced With |
|-------------|---------------|
| `{{PROJECT_ID}}` | Kebab-case ID (e.g. `s2morrow`) |
| `{{PROJECT_NAME}}` | Human-readable name (e.g. `S2morrow`) |
| `{{ROOT_RELATIVE}}` | The `--root` argument verbatim (e.g. `../..`) |
| `{{ROOT_URL_DEPTH}}` | Computed URL-relative path for `import.meta.url` resolution in `workflow.tsx` (adds one extra `../` because `workflow.tsx` is inside `components/`) |

### Side effects beyond file creation

1. **jj init** (unless `--no-jj`): Runs `jj git init --colocate` at the nearest git repo root if `.jj` doesn't already exist.
2. **bun install** (unless `--no-install`): Runs `bun install` inside the target directory to install `smithers-orchestrator` and `super-ralph`.

---

## What Needs Customization After Init

### 1. `components/focuses.ts` -- Replace placeholder focuses

The template ships with generic focuses (`core`, `api`, `storage`, `auth`, `testing`). You must replace these with 6-15 focus areas that map to your project's actual domain boundaries. Each focus drives:
- A CategoryReview audit per iteration
- An IntegrationTest suite per iteration
- A bucket for ticket discovery

### 2. `components/workflow.tsx` -- Fill in all TODO sections

This is the bulk of the customization. Specifically:

**Agent system prompts** (5 constants at the top of the file):
- `PLANNING_PROMPT` -- architecture rules, priority ordering, specs the agent must read
- `IMPLEMENTATION_PROMPT` -- mandatory/forbidden patterns, TDD requirements, build commands
- `TESTING_PROMPT` -- test runner, frameworks, mock patterns, coverage requirements
- `REVIEWING_PROMPT` -- review checklist, severity rules, spec compliance criteria
- `REPORTING_PROMPT` -- already has a reasonable default, may only need light tweaking

**SuperRalph props:**
- `specsPath` -- path to your spec/PRD documents (default is `"docs/specs/"`)
- `referenceFiles` -- array of paths agents should read for context (default is just `["README.md"]`)
- `buildCmds` -- record of named build commands (default is `typecheck` and `build` using `bun`)
- `testCmds` -- record of named test commands (default is `unit: "bun test"`)
- `codeStyle` -- string describing code style rules
- `reviewChecklist` -- array of checklist items for code/spec reviewers

**Agent configuration:**
- Choose agent types per role (`ClaudeCodeAgent`, `CodexAgent`, `GeminiAgent`, `KimiAgent`)
- Set models and timeouts per role
- Optionally set up fallback chains (arrays of agents for rate-limit resilience)

### 3. Optional advanced customization

Per the customization reference (`customization.md`), you can also optionally configure:
- `focusTestSuites` -- per-focus test suite hints (commands, setup hints, test directories)
- `focusDirs` -- per-focus directory hints for reviewers
- `preLandChecks` / `postLandChecks` -- checks before/after merge queue landing
- `maxSpeculativeDepth` -- merge queue speculation depth (default 3)

Based on the search results, here's information about the starknet.js Account `deployAccount` method and its parameters:

## deployAccount Method

The `deployAccount` method accepts parameters including `classHash`, `constructorCalldata` (defaults to an empty array), `addressSalt` (defaults to 0), and `contractAddress`.

## Key Parameters

- **classHash**: A contract class is identified by its Class Hash
- **constructorCalldata**: Constructor calldata can be compiled using CallData with the contract's ABI, specifying the constructor parameters as an object
- **addressSalt**: A salt value used for address derivation (defaults to 0)
- **contractAddress**: An optional pre-computed contract address

## How It Works

The method compiles the constructor calldata and calculates the contract address from the hash, address salt, and compiled calldata. When deploying an account with funds already sent to the pre-computed address, you can call `deployAccount` with the classHash, constructorCalldata, and addressSalt to deploy the new account.

## Transaction Details

The `DeployAccountContractTransaction` type includes an optional signature field, and the deploy account transaction hash is calculated using the contract address, classHash, constructorCalldata, salt, version, maxFee, chainId, and nonce.

Based on the search results, here's comprehensive information about setting up Tailwind CSS v4 with Next.js in 2025:

## Basic Setup for Next.js with Tailwind v4

Install @tailwindcss/postcss and its peer dependencies via npm. The installation command is:

npm install tailwindcss @tailwindcss/postcss postcss

## PostCSS Configuration

Create a postcss.config.mjs file in the root of your project and add the @tailwindcss/postcss plugin to your PostCSS configuration. Here's the configuration:

```javascript
export default {
  plugins: {
    '@tailwindcss/postcss': {},
  },
}
```

## Global CSS Setup

Add an @import to ./app/globals.css that imports Tailwind CSS. In your globals.css file, add:

```css
@import 'tailwindcss';
```

## Key Differences in v4

Tailwind CSS v4 was officially released on January 22, 2025, introducing several new features, optimizations, and a shift to a CSS-first configuration approach. Tailwind v4 emphasizes modern practices such as defining configurations directly in CSS and simplifies the integration process by replacing the need for postcss.config.js with @tailwindcss/postcss.

Tailwind v4 has simplified integration with Next, requiring fewer configuration steps.

## Note About Bun

The search results did not contain specific information about using Bun as a package manager with this setup. However, the installation commands should work similarly with `bun install` instead of `npm install` since Bun is compatible with npm packages.

# @mcrovero/effect-nextjs Package Summary ## Package Information - **Name**: `@mcrovero/effect-nextjs` - **Latest Version**: Available on [npm](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@mcrovero/effect-nextjs) - **License**: MIT ## Installation ```bash pnpm add @mcrovero/effect-nextjs effect ``` ## Key Purpose Write Next.js App Router pages, layouts, server components, and actions using Effect while preserving the familiar Next.js developer experience. ## Core API Exports **Runtime Creation**: - `Next.make(name, layer)` - Establishes a typed page handler with specified layers - `.build(handler)` - Compiles your Effect-based handler into a Next.js component - `.middleware(middlewareTag)` - Chains composable middleware for cross-cutting concerns **Middleware Framework**: - `NextMiddleware.Tag()` - Base class for defining typed middleware with success/failure schemas - Supports both standard and wrapped middlewares (`wrap: true`) **Navigation Utilities** (`@mcrovero/effect-nextjs/Navigation`): - `Redirect(path)` - Effect version of redirect - `PermanentRedirect(path)` - Effect version of permanent redirect - `NotFound` - Effect version of notFound **Cache Operations** (`@mcrovero/effect-nextjs/Cache`): - `RevalidatePath(path)` - `RevalidateTag(tag)` **Headers** (`@mcrovero/effect-nextjs/Headers`): - `Headers`, `Cookies`, `DraftMode` - Contextual access to request data **Route Parameters** (`@mcrovero/effect-nextjs/Params`): - `decodeParamsUnknown(schema)` - Validates dynamic route params with Effect Schema - `decodeSearchParamsUnknown(schema)` - Validates query parameters safely ## Basic Usage Pattern ```typescript const HomePage = Effect.fn("HomePage")(function* () { return
Hello World
}) export default BasePage.build(HomePage) ```

Based on the search results, I found comprehensive information about creating accounts with starknet.js, including the `deployAccount` and `calculateContractAddressFromHash` functions. Here's what the documentation shows:

## Account Creation Process

The process involves several steps: deciding on your account type (OpenZeppelin, ArgentX, Braavos, etc.), computing the address of your future account, sending funds to this pre-computed address, and the funds will be used to pay for the account contract deployment.

## Example Code Using `calculateContractAddressFromHash`

Here's an example using an ETH signer: create a signer with a private key, get the public key, compile constructor calldata with the public key, then use `hash.calculateContractAddressFromHash()` with the salt, account class hash, constructor calldata, and 0 as parameters to pre-calculate the account address.

Another example shows the ArgentX implementation: Generate a public and private key pair, compile constructor calldata with the owner and guardian parameters, then use `hash.calculateContractAddressFromHash()` with the public key as salt, the ArgentX account class hash, the compiled constructor calldata, and 0.

## Deploying the Account

After funding the pre-computed address, you can create an Account instance with the provider, contract address, and signer, then proceed to deploy.

## Practical Example

The deployment process involves creating an Account instance with the provider, address, and signer, then calling `deployAccount()` with the class hash, constructor calldata, address salt, and contract address parameters.

You can find the complete official guide on the Starknet.js documentation at https://starknetjs.com/docs/6.11.0/guides/create_account/


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Links: [{"title":"next.config.js: turbopack | Next.js","url":"https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/turbopack"},{"title":"API Reference: Turbopack | Next.js","url":"https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/turbopack"},{"title":"next.config.js: serverExternalPackages | Next.js","url":"https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/serverExternalPackages"},{"title":"Next.js 15 has been officially released! | Dev and Deliver","url":"https://devanddeliver.com/blog/frontend/next-js-15-has-been-officially-released"},{"title":"Loading .wasm files as assets with Turbopack · vercel/next.js · Discussion #75430","url":"https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/75430"},{"title":"Rust WebAssembly module in an ES module wrapper from wasm-pack fails to load in Next.js","url":"https://lightrun.com/answers/vercel-next-js-rust-webassembly-module-in-an-es-module-wrapper-from-wasm-pack-fails-to-load-in-nextjs"},{"title":"[turbopack]: importing `tailwind/tailwind.css` fails with `serverComponentsExternalPackages` error · Issue #64837 · vercel/next.js","url":"https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/64837"},{"title":"`next dev --turbo` unable to import ESM-only packages when marked with `serverComponentsExternalPackages` · Issue #64525 · vercel/next.js","url":"https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/64525"},{"title":"next.config.js Options: turbopack | Next.js","url":"https://nextjs.org/docs/pages/api-reference/config/next-config-js/turbopack"},{"title":"Next.js 15.3 | Next.js","url":"https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15-3"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found related to your query about Next.js 15, Turbopack, wasm-pack, and WebAssembly configuration:

## WebAssembly Support in Turbopack

Turbopack doesn't currently support custom WebAssembly (.wasm) handling via Webpack-like rules, but you can still import .wasm files using a manual fetch-based approach.

## Historical webpack Configuration

For reference, the traditional webpack approach with `asyncWebAssembly` experimental feature was used to enable WebAssembly support. Configuration involved setting `config.experiments = { asyncWebAssembly: true, layers: true }` in next.config.js along with custom webpack plugins for handling WebAssembly chunks.

## serverComponentsExternalPackages

The serverExternalPackages configuration is used to opt-out specific dependencies from Server Components bundling and use native Node.js `require`. However, there are known issues with Turbopack's handling of this configuration—when importing files that match serverComponentsExternalPackages, Turbopack may fail with the error "can't be external: Only .mjs, .cjs, .js, .json, or .node can be handled by Node.js."

## Current Status

Turbopack is now the default bundler in Next.js with zero-configuration for common use cases. For WebAssembly with wasm-pack in Next.js 15, the current workaround is to use manual fetch-based approaches rather than direct Webpack-like loader rules.

content.text:
Now I have all the information needed. Here is the complete research report.

---

## Research Results

### 1. `@prb/effect-next` -- Does NOT Exist

No package named `@prb/effect-next` exists on npm. The closest alternatives for Effect + Next.js integration are:

- **`@mcrovero/effect-nextjs`** -- Typed helpers for Next.js App Router pages, layouts, server components, and actions with Effect. Provides `Next.make()`, middleware, navigation utilities (`Redirect`, `NotFound`), cache operations, and `decodeParamsUnknown()` for typed route params. [GitHub](https://github.com/mcrovero/effect-nextjs)
- **`@effect/rpc-nextjs`** -- Official Effect RPC integration for Next.js (last published ~2 years ago, version 0.25.25; likely stale)

If someone referred to `@prb/effect-next`, they may have been thinking of `@mcrovero/effect-nextjs`.

---

### 2. Latest Package Versions

| Package | Latest Version | Notes |
|---------|---------------|-------|
| `next` | **16.1.6** | Next.js 16 shipped; Turbopack is default for dev + build |
| `effect` | **3.19.19** | The missing standard library for TypeScript |
| `@effect-atom/atom-react` | **0.5.0** | Renamed from `effect-rx`; reactive state for Effect + React |
| `@effect-atom/atom` | (companion) | Core atom primitives (install alongside atom-react) |
| `starknet` | **9.2.1** | starknet.js for Starknet interaction |
| `tailwindcss` | **4.2.0** | CSS-first config, no `tailwind.config.js` needed |
| `@tailwindcss/postcss` | (companion) | Required PostCSS plugin for v4 |

---

### 3. WASM Loading Patterns in Next.js 15/16

**Current state (Next.js 16 with Turbopack):**

Turbopack does not support Webpack's `experiments.asyncWebAssembly` directly. The recommended patterns are:

**Pattern A: Copy `.wasm` to `public/` and instantiate at runtime**
```typescript
// lib/falcon-wasm.ts
let wasmModule: typeof import("../pkg/falcon_rs");

export async function getFalcon() {
  if (!wasmModule) {
    const wasm = await import("../pkg/falcon_rs");
    await wasm.default();  // calls __wbg_init with fetch to .wasm
    wasmModule = wasm;
  }
  return wasmModule;
}
```

**Pattern B: Webpack fallback config (for non-Turbopack builds)**
```javascript
// next.config.mjs
const nextConfig = {
  webpack(config) {
    config.experiments = { ...config.experiments, asyncWebAssembly: true };
    config.output.webassemblyModuleFilename = "static/wasm/[modulehash].wasm";
    return config;
  },
};
```

**Pattern C: `wasm-pack` build command**
```bash
wasm-pack build ../falcon-rs --target web --out-dir ../s2morrow/pkg/falcon-rs
```
This generates `falcon_rs.js` + `falcon_rs_bg.wasm` that can be loaded client-side.

For Next.js 16 with Turbopack, **Pattern A** (copy to `public/` + dynamic `import()`) is the most reliable.

---

### 4. starknet.js Account Deployment API

The account deployment flow in starknet.js v9:

```typescript
import {
  Account,
  RpcProvider,
  hash,
  CallData,
  stark,
  ec,
} from "starknet";

// 1. Generate keypair
const privateKey = stark.randomAddress();
const publicKey = ec.starkCurve.getStarkKey(privateKey);

// 2. Compute future address
const accountClassHash = "0x...";  // Your falcon account class hash
const constructorCalldata = CallData.compile({ public_key: publicKey });
const contractAddress = hash.calculateContractAddressFromHash(
  publicKey,           // addressSalt
  accountClassHash,    // classHash
  constructorCalldata, // constructor calldata
  0                    // deployer address (0 for deploy_account)
);

// 3. Fund the pre-computed address (send ETH/STRK to contractAddress)

// 4. Deploy the account
const provider = new RpcProvider({ nodeUrl: "https://..." });
const account = new Account(provider, contractAddress, privateKey);
const { transaction_hash, contract_address } = await account.deployAccount({
  classHash: accountClassHash,
  constructorCalldata,
  addressSalt: publicKey,
  contractAddress,
});

await provider.waitForTransaction(transaction_hash);
```

Key method signatures:
- `account.deployAccount({ classHash, constructorCalldata, addressSalt, contractAddress })` returns `{ transaction_hash, contract_address }`
- `hash.calculateContractAddressFromHash(salt, classHash, calldata, deployerAddress)` returns the pre-computed address

---

### 5. falcon-rs Public API

Located at `/home/felt/PycharmProjects/falcon-rs/`.

**Cargo.toml** -- crate type is `["cdylib", "rlib"]` with features:
- `default = ["shake"]` -- SHAKE256 hash + system RNG
- `wasm = ["wasm-bindgen", "js-sys", "getrandom/js", "shake"]` -- WASM bindings

**Core types** (from `/home/felt/PycharmProjects/falcon-rs/src/falcon.rs`):

```rust
pub struct Falcon<H: HashToPoint> { ... }
pub struct SecretKey { f, g, capital_f, capital_g, b0_fft, tree }
pub struct VerifyingKey { h: [i32; 512] }
pub struct Signature { header: u8, salt: [u8; 40], s1_enc: Vec<u8> }

pub const PUBLIC_KEY_LEN: usize = 896;
pub const SIGNATURE_LEN: usize = 666;
```

**Public methods on `Falcon<H>`**:
| Method | Signature | Notes |
|--------|-----------|-------|
| `keygen()` | `() -> (SecretKey, VerifyingKey)` | Requires `shake` feature |
| `keygen_with_seed(seed)` | `(&[u8]) -> (SecretKey, VerifyingKey)` | Deterministic |
| `keygen_with_rng(rng)` | `(&mut FnMut) -> (SecretKey, VerifyingKey)` | Custom RNG |
| `sign(sk, msg)` | `(&SecretKey, &[u8]) -> Signature` | Requires `shake`, random salt |
| `sign_with_salt(sk, msg, salt)` | `(&SecretKey, &[u8], &[u8; 40]) -> Signature` | Deterministic |
| `sign_prehashed(sk, hash_point, seed)` | `(&SecretKey, &[i16; 512], &[u8; 56]) -> (Vec<i32>, Vec<i32>)` | For Poseidon hash-to-point |
| `verify(vk, msg, sig)` | `(&VerifyingKey, &[u8], &Signature) -> Result<bool, FalconError>` | |

**Hash-to-point implementations** (from `/home/felt/PycharmProjects/falcon-rs/src/hash_to_point.rs` and `/home/felt/PycharmProjects/falcon-rs/src/poseidon_hash.rs`):
- `Shake256Hash` -- standard byte-oriented SHAKE256
- `PoseidonHashToPoint` -- Starknet-compatible Poseidon (uses lambdaworks)

**WASM bindings** (from `/home/felt/PycharmProjects/falcon-rs/src/wasm.rs`) -- currently exposes:

| Function | JS Signature | Status |
|----------|-------------|--------|
| `keygen(seed: Uint8Array)` | Returns `{ sk: Uint8Array, vk: Uint8Array }` | Working |
| `sign(sk_bytes, message, seed)` | Returns `Vec<u8>` | **NOT IMPLEMENTED** (stub) |
| `verify(vk_bytes, message, signature)` | Returns `boolean` | Working (SHAKE256 only) |
| `public_key_length()` | Returns `896` | Working |
| `salt_length()` | Returns `40` | Working |

**Additional modules**:
- `hints.rs` -- `generate_mul_hint(s1: &[u16], pk_h_ntt: &[u16]) -> Vec<u16>` for Cairo hint-based verification
- `packing.rs` -- `pack_public_key(h_ntt: &[u16]) -> Vec<Felt>`, `unpack_public_key(packed: &[Felt]) -> Vec<u16>` (29 felt252 slots)
- `ntt.rs` -- `ntt`, `intt`, `mul_ntt`, `div_zq`, `mul_zq`, `sub_zq`
- `serialize.rs` -- `hash_to_point_test_json`, `packing_test_json`, `verify_test_json` for snforge test data generation

---

### 6. Recommended Setup Commands

```bash
# Initialize Next.js 16 project with Bun
bun create next-app s2morrow-app --ts --tailwind --eslint --app --src-dir

# Or, if adding to existing project:
bun add next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest

# Effect ecosystem
bun add effect @effect-atom/atom @effect-atom/atom-react

# Effect + Next.js integration (pick one)
bun add @mcrovero/effect-nextjs   # typed App Router helpers

# Starknet
bun add starknet@^9.2.1

# Tailwind CSS v4 (if not already from create-next-app)
bun add tailwindcss @tailwindcss/postcss postcss

# WASM tooling (dev)
bun add -d wasm-pack  # or install globally via: curl https://rustwasm.github.io/wasm-pack/installer/init.sh -sSf | sh

# Build falcon-rs WASM
cd /home/felt/PycharmProjects/falcon-rs
wasm-pack build --target web --out-dir /home/felt/PycharmProjects/s2morrow/pkg/falcon-rs --features wasm
```

**Tailwind v4 CSS-first config** -- no `tailwind.config.js` needed:

`postcss.config.mjs`:
```javascript
export default {
  plugins: {
    "@tailwindcss/postcss": {},
  },
};
```

`app/globals.css`:
```css
@import "tailwindcss";
```

---

### Sources

- [next - npm](https://www.npmjs.com/package/next)
- [effect - npm](https://www.npmjs.com/package/effect)
- [@effect-atom/atom-react - npm](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@effect-atom/atom-react)
- [starknet - npm](https://www.npmjs.com/package/starknet)
- [tailwindcss - npm](https://www.npmjs.com/package/tailwindcss)
- [mcrovero/effect-nextjs - GitHub](https://github.com/mcrovero/effect-nextjs)
- [Tim Smart on X: renamed effect-rx to effect-atom](https://x.com/tim_smart/status/1953754224958878039)
- [Tailwind CSS v4 PostCSS installation](https://tailwindcss.com/docs/installation/using-postcss)
- [Install Tailwind CSS with Next.js](https://tailwindcss.com/docs/guides/nextjs)
- [Loading .wasm files as assets with Turbopack - Next.js Discussion](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/75430)
- [vite-plugin-wasm - GitHub](https://github.com/Menci/vite-plugin-wasm)
- [Create an account | Starknet.js](https://starknetjs.com/docs/6.11.0/guides/create_account/)
- [Starknet.js API v9.2.1](https://starknetjs.com/docs/API/modules/)
- [Next.js 16 Blog](https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16)
